title: Chicken Chasseur

ingredients.name: chicken pieces, bone-in (thighs or mixed), skin-on

subs.name: 500 g mutton or lamb pieces — increase braising time to 45 minutes
subs.notes: the sauce flavours work equally well with red meat

subs.name: 3 tbsp tomato paste dissolved in 150 ml water — gives a more concentrated, deeper flavour
subs.notes: reduce the stock by 50 ml if using paste

subs.name: 250 ml water with a Knorr or Maggi vegetable stock cube dissolved in it
ghee — adds a richer, nuttier finish
subs.notes: use the same quantity; stir in at the very end

steps.text: Pat the chicken pieces completely dry with kitchen paper — moisture prevents browning. Season all sides generously with salt and pepper.

Heat the oil in a wide, heavy kadai or heavy-bottomed pan over high flame. Place the chicken pieces skin-side down and sear without moving for 5 minutes until the skin is deep golden. Flip and sear the other side for 3 minutes. Transfer the browned chicken to a plate.
Reduce the heat to medium. Add the onion to the fat remaining in the pan and sauté for 4 minutes until translucent, scraping up any browned bits from the base. Add the garlic and cook 1 minute more.
Add the mushrooms and cook for 5–6 minutes, stirring occasionally, until they release their liquid and the pan is nearly dry. They should take on a little colour at the edges.
Add the chopped tomatoes and cook for 3 minutes, pressing them with the back of a spoon until they collapse into the pan juices.

Return the browned chicken pieces to the pan, fitting them snugly. Pour in the stock and tuck in the thyme sprigs and bay leaves. Bring to a brisk simmer, then cover tightly and reduce the flame to medium-low.

Braise covered for 25 minutes, turning the chicken once halfway through, until the meat is cooked through and pulls away from the bone easily. If the sauce seems thin, uncover and simmer 5 more minutes to concentrate.

Remove the thyme stalks and bay leaves. Stir in the butter and taste for seasoning. Scatter the chopped parsley over the top and serve directly from the pan with steamed rice, mashed potato, or crusty bread.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: chicken → 500 g mutton or lamb pieces — increase braising time to 45 minutes (the sauce flavours work equally well with red meat); butter → ghee — adds a richer, nuttier finish (use the same quantity; stir in at the very end); stock → 250 ml water with a Knorr or Maggi vegetable stock cube dissolved in it (perfectly acceptable result); tomato → 3 tbsp tomato paste dissolved in 150 ml water — gives a more concentrated, deeper flavour (reduce the stock by 50 ml if using paste)
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A kadai is ideal for this entire recipe — the shape encourages browning and the depth allows the braise. The technique (sear-then-braise) mirrors many Indian curries and feels natural on an Indian gas burner. Use desi chicken (country chicken) for the best flavour and texture; the longer connective tissue suits the braising method. A pressure cooker can be used: after step 6, cook under pressure for 2 whistles on medium, then reduce uncovered for 5 minutes.
